7.note: Those who do not confess the coming of Jesus Christ in the flesh are the deceiver and the antichrist — the same anti-Docetic test as 1 John 4:2 (every spirit that confesses Jesus Christ has come in the flesh is from God); 2 John applies the same doctrinal test to traveling teachers

1.note: The 'elect lady' (eklekte kyria) is most likely a metaphorical address for a local congregation, paralleling 1 Peter's greeting to 'she who is in Babylon, who is likewise chosen' (eklekte) — the church addressed as an elect woman; both letters use the feminine to address the community as the Bride of Christ

The elder's identical self-designation (ho presbyteros) in both 2 John and 3 John ties the two letters to the same author and authority structure; 3 John addresses an individual (Gaius) while 2 John addresses a congregation, forming a diptych of community instruction

2.note: The truth 'abiding in us' (menousan en hemin) echoes the Paraclete's abiding: 'He abides with you and will be in you' (John 14:17); the Spirit of truth and the propositional truth of the gospel are inseparable in Johannine theology — to hold the truth is to host the Spirit

The truth 'will be with us forever' (eis ton aiona) parallels 1 John 2:17: 'Whoever does the will of God abides forever' — permanence belongs to what is of God; the deceivers' doctrine, by contrast, passes away

3.note: The triple greeting 'grace, mercy, and peace' (charis eleos eirene) matches Paul's pastoral greeting formula in 1 Timothy 1:2 and 2 Timothy 1:2, distinguishing the Pastoral and General Epistles' greetings from the standard Pauline 'grace and peace' — mercy added because the pastoral context involves vulnerable communities needing God's tenderness

Jesus Christ as 'the Son of the Father' (ton huion tou patros) — the relational filial identity central to John 3:16-17; the greeting's Christology (Sonship, divine origin) is exactly what the deceivers of v7 deny: they cannot affirm 'the Son of the Father' coming in flesh

4.note: Near-verbatim parallel: 'I have no greater joy than to hear that my children are walking in the truth' (3 John 4) — the elder uses identical joy-language for both communities; walking in truth is the Johannine idiom for whole-life gospel embodiment

Walking in truth (peripatounta en aletheia) activates John 8:32 ('the truth will set you free') — truth is not merely intellectual but a path of life; those who walk in it fulfill the Father's commandment (v4b) and contrast with the deceivers who have 'gone out into the world' (v7)

5.note: The commandment 'that we love one another' quotes Jesus's new commandment at the Last Supper (John 13:34); 2 John insists it is not new (from the beginning, v5) because it recapitulates the Mosaic love-commandment (Leviticus 19:18) — Jesus fulfills rather than replaces; Johannine 'new' = eschatologically empowered, not historically unprecedented

1 John 2:7 uses the same rhetorical structure: 'I am writing you no new commandment, but an old commandment that you had from the beginning' — the elder's consistent teaching on love as both ancient and renewed in Christ

6.note: Defining love as walking in God's commandments (v6) is the same definition as 1 John 5:3: 'For this is the love of God, that we keep his commandments' — love and obedience are the same motion in Johannine ethics; feeling without walking is not love

Walking in commandments from the beginning traces to the Shema's covenant obligation (Deuteronomy 6:5, 'you shall love the LORD your God'); 2 John's community ethic is the Shema's love-commandment now christologically defined — loving God means walking in Christ's teaching

8.note: The warning 'watch yourselves' (blepete heautous) so that the community does not lose what it has worked for echoes Jesus's eschatological warning 'the one who endures to the end will be saved' (Matthew 24:13) — apostasy under false teaching is the danger 2 John guards against

The appeal to receive a 'full reward' (misthon plere) parallels Revelation 3:11's warning to the Philadelphia church: 'Hold fast what you have, so that no one may seize your crown' — faithfulness preserves the eschatological reward; the reward is jeopardized by receiving false teachers (vv9-11)

9.note: Whoever denies the Son does not have the Father (1 John 2:23) is the doctrinal principle behind 2 John 9: 'Whoever goes ahead and does not abide in the teaching of Christ does not have God' — Christological confession is the gateway to Trinitarian fellowship; Docetism cuts the believer off from the Father

Jesus as the only way to the Father (John 14:6) underlies the binary: abide in Christ's teaching = have both Father and Son; abandon it = have neither; there is no access to God apart from the teaching of the incarnate Son

10.note: Paul's instruction to 'note those who cause divisions and create obstacles contrary to the doctrine you have been taught, and avoid them' (Romans 16:17) is the same protective principle as 2 John 10 applied to hospitality: the community's doctrinal integrity requires limiting fellowship that would validate false teaching

The graduated church discipline of Matthew 18:15-17 ends with treating the unrepentant offender 'as a Gentile and a tax collector' — exclusion from table fellowship; 2 John 10 extends this to hospitality itself, denying the traveling false teacher the platform of the household church

11.note: Take no part in the unfruitful works of darkness (Ephesians 5:11) is the Pauline parallel to 2 John 11's 'whoever greets him takes part in his evil works' (koinonei tois ergois autou tois ponerois) — participation through endorsement; hospitality to false teachers is complicity in the damage they do to congregations

Paul's instruction not to associate with those who claim the name of Christ but live in ongoing sin (1 Corinthians 5:11) establishes the same principle as 2 John 11: fellowship-limits are not personal rejection but protection of the community's doctrinal integrity and the false teacher from further hardening

12.note: The desire that 'our joy may be complete' (he chara hemon pepleromen e) matches 1 John 1:4 verbatim — the elder's letters share the same closing purpose: full joy realized in face-to-face community; written communication is provisional, incarnate presence is complete

3 John 13-14 is the near-identical closing: 'I had much to write to you, but I would rather not write with pen and ink. I hope to see you soon, and we will talk face to face' — both letters close with the same deferrral from writing to presence; the literary form mirrors the theological content (embodied presence over disembodied word)

13.note: The closing greeting from 'the children of your elect sister' mirrors 1 Peter 5:13's closing from 'she who is in Babylon' — both letters use the elect/chosen feminine to close community-to-community greetings; the church in one place greets the church in another as sisters in election

Greet one another with a holy kiss; all the churches of Christ greet you (Romans 16:16) — the closing community-greetings in Paul and in 2-3 John express the same ecclesial consciousness: local churches are members of one body across geography, bound by shared election and shared doctrine
